passage.title: The Breaking Point of an Enzyme
passage.body_md:
Students investigated the activity of *catalase*, an enzyme that speeds the breakdown of hydrogen peroxide (H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub>) into water and oxygen gas (O<sub>2</sub>):

2 H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub> → 2 H<sub>2</sub>O + O<sub>2</sub>

Catalase extracted from beef liver was added to a fixed volume of H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub> solution, and the volume of O<sub>2</sub> gas released in 2 minutes was collected and measured. A greater volume of O<sub>2</sub> indicates greater catalase activity. The same mass of catalase and the same starting amount of H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub> were used in every trial.

**Experiment 1**

The pH of the H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub> solution was held constant at 7.0. The temperature of the solution was varied, and the O<sub>2</sub> released was measured.

| Temperature (°C) | O<sub>2</sub> produced in 2 min (mL) |
|---|---|
| 10 | 8 |
| 20 | 16 |
| 30 | 28 |
| 40 | 42 |
| 50 | 24 |
| 60 | 6 |

**Experiment 2**

The temperature was held constant at 40°C. The pH of the H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub> solution was varied, and the O<sub>2</sub> released was measured.

| pH | O<sub>2</sub> produced in 2 min (mL) |
|---|---|
| 3 | 5 |
| 5 | 20 |
| 7 | 42 |
| 9 | 22 |
| 11 | 4 |

**Experiment 3**

Separate catalase samples were each pre-incubated (held) for 10 minutes at one of four temperatures. Each sample was then brought to 40°C and added to H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub> solution at pH 7.0, and the O<sub>2</sub> released in 2 minutes was measured.

| Pre-incubation temperature (°C) | O<sub>2</sub> produced at 40°C in 2 min (mL) |
|---|---|
| 10 | 42 |
| 40 | 42 |
| 50 | 30 |
| 60 | 8 |

questions.stem_md: According to the results of Experiment 1, catalase activity, as measured by the volume of O<sub>2</sub> produced, was greatest at which temperature?
questions.explanation_md: The table for Experiment 1 shows the largest O<sub>2</sub> volume, 42 mL, at 40°C. At 20°C only 16 mL was produced, at 30°C only 28 mL, and at 50°C the volume had fallen to 24 mL, so none of those temperatures gave the greatest activity.

questions.stem_md: In Experiment 2, as the pH of the H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub> solution was increased from 3 to 7, the volume of O<sub>2</sub> produced:
questions.explanation_md: Over the range from pH 3 to pH 7 the volume rose steadily from 5 mL to 20 mL to 42 mL, so it increased only. It did not decrease or stay constant, and the decrease (to 22 mL and then 4 mL) happened only after pH 7, which is outside the range asked about.

questions.stem_md: Based on the results of Experiment 1, if a trial had been performed at 35°C, the volume of O<sub>2</sub> produced in 2 minutes would most likely have been closest to:
questions.explanation_md: A temperature of 35°C lies between 30°C (28 mL) and 40°C (42 mL), so the expected value falls between those, near 35 mL. Values of 8 mL and 20 mL correspond to much lower temperatures, and 50 mL is greater than any volume actually recorded in the experiment.

questions.stem_md: In Experiment 2 the temperature was held constant at 40°C. The most likely reason the students chose 40°C is that this temperature:
choices: produced the greatest catalase activity in Experiment 1, making effects of pH easier to detect
was the lowest temperature tested in Experiment 1
was equal to the pH used in Experiment 1
prevented the enzyme from working at any pH
questions.explanation_md: Experiment 1 showed activity peaked at 40°C, so running Experiment 2 there gives the strongest response and makes pH effects clearest. 40°C was the peak, not the lowest, temperature tested; temperature (°C) and pH are different quantities, so they cannot be "equal"; and the enzyme clearly did work at 40°C, producing up to 42 mL of O<sub>2</sub>.

questions.stem_md: A student claims that catalase functions best under conditions similar to those inside the human body (about 37°C and near pH 7). Do the results of Experiments 1 and 2 support this claim?
choices: Yes; peak activity occurred near 40°C and near pH 7, close to body conditions
Yes; peak activity occurred near 10°C and near pH 3
No; peak activity occurred near 60°C and near pH 11
No; activity was the same at every temperature and pH tested
questions.explanation_md: Activity peaked at 40°C in Experiment 1 and at pH 7 in Experiment 2, both close to the stated body conditions, so the data support the claim. Activity was low, not peak, at 10°C, pH 3, 60°C, and pH 11, and it clearly varied with both temperature and pH rather than staying the same.

questions.stem_md: In Experiment 3 the samples were pre-incubated at different temperatures but all tested at 40°C. These results best support which conclusion about the drop in activity seen at 50°C and 60°C in Experiment 1?
choices: The high temperatures caused permanent damage to the enzyme (denaturation)
The reaction was simply too slow because 50°C and 60°C are too cold
The H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub> was completely used up before the enzyme was added
Catalase works equally well at all temperatures
questions.explanation_md: Samples pre-incubated at 60°C produced only 8 mL even when later tested at the optimal 40°C, showing the high temperature permanently damaged (denatured) the enzyme rather than just slowing it temporarily. 50°C and 60°C are hot, not cold; the same H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub> starting amount was used each time; and activity clearly differed across temperatures, so catalase does not work equally well at all of them.

questions.stem_md: Suppose a fresh catalase sample is first heated to 60°C for 10 minutes and then placed in H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub> solution at 40°C and pH 7. Based on all three experiments, the volume of O<sub>2</sub> produced in 2 minutes would most likely be closest to:
questions.explanation_md: Experiment 3 shows that a sample pre-heated to 60°C produced only 8 mL even when later tested at the ideal 40°C and pH 7, because the heat had already denatured it. The 42 mL and 30 mL values correspond to samples that were never pre-heated to 60°C, and 0 mL is too low, since the damaged enzyme still produced a small, measurable amount of O<sub>2</sub>.
